1: τῇ ἐλευθερίᾳ ἡμᾶς Χριστὸς ἠλευθέρωσεν (for freedom Christ freed us, G1659) — the dative tē eleutheria is probably a dative of purpose: Christ freed us for freedom. The cognate construction (eleutheria... ēleutherōsen) places freedom at both the beginning and the end: freedom is both the means and the goal. The aorist ēleutherōsen marks a definitive past act. στήκετε οὖν (stand firm therefore, G4739) — present imperative: continue standing. μὴ πάλιν ζυγῷ δουλείας ἐνέχεσθε (do not be entangled again in a yoke of slavery, G1758) — enechesthe (be entangled, be held in) is present passive imperative: stop being held in.
2: ἴδε ἐγὼ Παῦλος λέγω ὑμῖν (behold, I Paul tell you) — the emphatic personal assertion, using the same formula as the oath of 1:20; Paul stakes his personal authority on what follows. ὅτι ἐὰν περιτέμνησθε, Χριστὸς ὑμᾶς οὐδὲν ὠφελήσει (that if you are circumcised, Christ will benefit you nothing) — the future ōphelēsei (will benefit) is a statement of theological consequence, not a curse: choosing circumcision as the means of covenant standing removes one from the domain where Christ's benefit operates.
3: πάντι ἀνθρώπῳ περιτεμνομένῳ ὅτι ὀφειλέτης ἐστὶν ὅλον τὸν νόμον ποιῆσαι (to every person who lets himself be circumcised that he is obligated to do the whole law) — the present passive participle; accepting circumcision as a covenant obligation activates the entire legal system, not a single ritual. The law is not partial: one cannot selectively adopt its benefits without accepting its comprehensive demands.
4: κατηργήθητε ἀπὸ Χριστοῦ (you were severed/separated from Christ, G2673) — the aorist passive; the same katargeō verb Paul uses for the law being "nullified" is here used for severance from Christ. The irony: those seeking to add the law to their Christ-faith are severed from Christ. ἐκ χάριτος ἐξεπέσατε (you fell from grace, G1601) — the aorist of ekpiptō (to fall out of); grace is a domain and system from which one can exit by shifting to a law-basis.
5: ἡμεῖς γὰρ πνεύματι ἐκ πίστεως ἐλπίδα δικαιοσύνης ἀπεκδεχόμεθα (for we, by the Spirit, from/through faith await the hope of righteousness) — the two instruments (pneumati and ek pisteōs) specify how the awaiting happens: by the Spirit and through faith. Elpida dikaiosynēs (hope of righteousness) probably refers to the final eschatological verdict — the full realization of justification at the Last Day — rather than the present imputed status. The present tense apekdechometha (we keep on waiting) is the posture of those who live between the already and the not-yet.
6: ἐν γὰρ Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ οὔτε περιτομή τι ἰσχύει οὔτε ἀκροβυστία (in Christ Jesus neither circumcision nor uncircumcision has any power, G2480) — the sphere en Christō Iēsou is the domain where the ethnic markers cease to be operative; within that domain, the only thing that matters is specified next. ἀλλὰ πίστις δι᾽ ἀγάπης ἐνεργουμένη (but faith working through love, G1754) — the present middle/passive participle; faith that is activated through love, or faith with love as the instrument of its activation. Love is not added to faith as a co-ground of salvation but describes the operative mode of genuine faith.
7: ἐτρέχετε καλῶς (you were running well, imperfect) — the progressive imperfect describes the previous sustained good running; the athletic metaphor indicates forward progress now interrupted. τίς ὑμᾶς ἐνέκοψεν (who cut in on you, G1465) — the aorist of enkoptō (to cut into, interrupt): someone has cut across their lane. The unnamed agitator is the one who interrupted the race.
8: ἡ πεισμονὴ οὐκ ἐκ τοῦ καλοῦντος ὑμᾶς (the persuasion is not from the one who calls you) — peismonē (G3988) is a hapax in the NT: persuasion, the quality of being persuaded. The persuasion the Galatians are following does not originate from the one who called them into Christ's grace (1:6).
9: μικρὰ ζύμη ὅλον τὸ φύραμα ζυμοῖ (a little yeast works through the whole batch of dough, G2219) — the proverbial statement (also 1 Cor 5:6) about the pervading power of small quantities; the principle applies to the law-adding agitation: a small doctrinal corruption spreads through the whole community.
10: ἐγὼ πέποιθα εἰς ὑμᾶς ἐν κυρίῳ (I am persuaded about you in the Lord) — the perfect pepoitha (I have been persuaded and remain persuaded): a standing confidence Paul maintains. ὁ δὲ ταράσσων ὑμᾶς βαστάσει τὸ κρίμα (but the one troubling you will bear the judgment, G941) — the singular ho tarassōn (the one troubling) individualizes the source of the trouble; bastasei to krima (will bear the judgment): the future tense points to eschatological accountability.
11: τὸ σκάνδαλον τοῦ σταυροῦ (the stumbling block of the cross, G4625/G4716) — if Paul were preaching circumcision, the offense of the cross would be eliminated; the cross as skandalon is precisely the stumbling block to those seeking a righteousness they can claim credit for. The cross eliminates all human achievement-grounds; that is its scandal.
12: ὄφελον καὶ ἀποκόψονται οἱ ἀναστατοῦντες ὑμᾶς (I wish those agitating you would go the whole way and castrate themselves) — the sarcastic irony: if circumcision is so important, why stop there? The rhetorical brutality serves the shock value of exposing the body-focused logic of the agitators by following it to its absurd conclusion. Apokopto (G609) means to cut off entirely.
13: ὑμεῖς γὰρ ἐπ᾽ ἐλευθερίᾳ ἐκλήθητε, ἀδελφοί (for you were called to freedom, brothers, G1657) — the aorist passive marks the definitive past calling; freedom is the domain into which the call brought them. μόνον μὴ τὴν ἐλευθερίαν εἰς ἀφορμὴν τῇ σαρκί (only do not use freedom as an opportunity for the flesh, G4561 disp=4) — aphormē (G874, base of operations, opportunity): freedom must not become a base from which the flesh launches its campaigns.
14: ὁ γὰρ πᾶς νόμος ἐν ἑνὶ λόγῳ πεπλήρωται (for the whole law is fulfilled in one word/commandment, G4137) — the perfect passive peplērōtai: a completed fulfillment that stands. The articular ho pas nomos (the whole law, with article) emphasizes comprehensiveness. ἀγαπήσεις τὸν πλησίον σου ὡς σεαυτόν (you shall love your neighbor as yourself, Lev 19:18) — the neighbor-love command from Leviticus as the law's single comprehensive form.
15: εἰ δὲ ἀλλήλους δάκνετε καὶ κατεσθίετε (but if you bite and devour each other) — the animal-predation metaphor (daknō = bite; katesthiō = eat up/devour) describes the communal violence of competitive religious performance. βλέπετε μὴ ὑπ᾽ ἀλλήλων ἀναλωθῆτε (watch out lest you be consumed by each other) — the passive indicates mutual destruction through the very conflict that religious law-competition produces.
16: πνεύματι περιπατεῖτε (walk by the Spirit, G4151 disp=3) — the present imperative: an ongoing, habitual practice. The dative pneumati is instrumental: walking with the Spirit as the means and standard. ἐπιθυμίαν σαρκὸς οὐ μὴ τελέσητε (you will absolutely not fulfill the desire of the flesh) — the double negative (ou mē) plus aorist subjunctive is the strongest possible negation: you will under no circumstances fulfill the flesh-desire.
17: ἡ γὰρ σὰρξ ἐπιθυμεῖ κατὰ τοῦ πνεύματος, τὸ δὲ πνεῦμα κατὰ τῆς σαρκός (for the flesh desires against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh, G4561 disp=4/G4151 disp=3) — the mutual opposition is cosmic and structural; both sarx and pneuma use the same verb epithumeō (desire toward, desire against). ταῦτα γὰρ ἀλλήλοις ἀντίκειται (for these are opposed to one another, G480) — the present tense describes an ongoing structural opposition, not occasional conflict.
18: εἰ δὲ πνεύματι ἄγεσθε (but if you are led by the Spirit) — the present passive: being led is something happening to the person, not self-generated. οὐκ ἐστὲ ὑπὸ νόμον (you are not under the law) — the Spirit-led are not under the law because the Spirit is fulfilling the law's righteous requirement in them (cf. Rom 8:4); the law's guardianship function is superseded by the Spirit's direct leading.
19: φανερὰ δέ ἐστιν τὰ ἔργα τῆς σαρκός (the works of the flesh are obvious, G4561 disp=4) — phanera (manifest, evident): no special spiritual insight is needed to identify them. ἔργα (works) — the plural underlines the productivity of the flesh-principle; it generates a variety of destructive outputs.
20: εἰδωλολατρία, φαρμακεία (idolatry, sorcery/witchcraft) — pharamakeia (G5331) is the manipulation of spiritual powers through substances or rituals; both practices involve the replacement of God with alternative ultimate authorities. The social vices that follow (hatred, discord, jealousy, etc.) are equally "works of the flesh."
21: βασιλείαν θεοῦ οὐ κληρονομήσουσιν (will not inherit the kingdom of God) — the future with absolute negative: those who practice (prassontes, present participle of habit) these things are excluding themselves from the inheritance that Paul has been arguing is available through Christ and the Spirit. The warning is to those who continue in such practices, not to those who struggle against them.
22: ὁ δὲ καρπὸς τοῦ πνεύματος (but the fruit of the Spirit) — the singular karpos (fruit, G2590) contrasts with the plural erga (works) of v.19; the Spirit produces a unified, organic fruit rather than a catalog of separate achievements. The nine qualities listed are a single harvest, not a checklist. ἀγάπη χαρὰ εἰρήνη (love, joy, peace) — the first triad is relational; the second (patience, kindness, goodness) is dispositional; the third (faithfulness, gentleness, self-control) is volitional.
23: κατὰ τῶν τοιούτων οὐκ ἔστιν νόμος (against such things there is no law) — the dry understatement: no law prohibits the fruit of the Spirit. The law was given for restraining transgression; where the Spirit's fruit flourishes, the law's restraining function has no application.
24: οἱ δὲ τοῦ Χριστοῦ Ἰησοῦ τὴν σάρκα ἐσταύρωσαν (those who belong to Christ Jesus have crucified the flesh, G4717) — the possessive genitive tou Christou (belonging to Christ); the aorist estaurōsan marks a definitive past act, not an ongoing process: the crucifixion of the flesh is a completed event (cf. 2:20 "I have been crucified with Christ"). σὺν τοῖς παθήμασιν καὶ ταῖς ἐπιθυμίαις (with its passions and desires) — the flesh is crucified along with its characteristic outputs: the drives and desires that the vices of vv.19-21 express.
25: εἰ ζῶμεν πνεύματι, πνεύματι καὶ στοιχῶμεν (if we live by the Spirit, let us also walk in step with the Spirit, G4748) — stoicheō (G4748) means to march in a row, keep in step with a line; the metaphor is military/processional rather than the general "walk" (peripateō) of v.16. The subjunctive stoichōmen is a hortatory call to maintain alignment with the Spirit whose life one already has.
26: μὴ γινώμεθα κενόδοξοι (let us not become vainglorious, G2755) — kenodoxos = empty-glory-seeking; the present subjunctive ginōmetha is an ongoing prohibition: let us not become this. The three negative community-behaviors (empty boasting, provoking one another, envying) are the social pathologies that the flesh produces when the community forgets its shared Spirit-life.
